notes: Single source of truth for candidate venues. The script reads THIS file to decide what to query (weather + flights). 'travel' gives how each person reaches the venue: mode fly|local|drive and the destination airport IATA they fly into (Michel from London, Dan from Belfast).

venues.name: Fair Head, NI
venues.why: The biggest expanse of climbable rock in Ireland — a sweep of towering dolerite columns above the sea, stacked with steep, juggy crack lines from VS upward. Dan lives down the road, so logistics are trivial: no hire car, no faff, just racks and the Causeway coast. Catches sea fog, but a breeze dries it fast.

venues.name: Mournes, NI
venues.why: Compact granite domes and tors an hour from Belfast — friction slabs, clean cracks and long mountain days with the sea in view from every summit. Local to Dan, moderate grades, and a solid choice when the forecast only gives a short two-day window.

venues.name: Dolomites (Cortina)
venues.why: The confirmed backup if Ireland is wet: vast limestone faces around Cortina with multi-pitch routes of every length and grade, and rifugi that put you at the base of the climbs. Late July is peak season — warm rock and settled mornings, with the classic afternoon-thunderstorm rhythm to plan around. Routes already scoped in dolomites-trip.csv.

venues.name: East Tyrol (Lienz)
venues.why: The quieter alpine alternative — Dolomite-style limestone above the Drau valley without Cortina's crowds, and the same settled high-summer window. Big medium-to-hard multi-pitch with easy valley logistics from Lienz.

venues.name: Lake District (Borrowdale)
venues.why: Borrowdale packs classic mountain routes at every grade within minutes of the road — perfect for pouncing on a dry spell. Drivable for Michel, cheap, sociable, and Keswick's drying rooms are legendary for a reason. In climbing-trips.csv.

venues.name: Snowdonia (Llanberis Pass)
venues.why: The home of classic UK mountain trad: Dinas Cromlech's open-book corners, Cloggy's big serious faces, Tryfan's endless ridges. Drivable and cheap — but rhyolite is slow to dry, so it needs a genuine dry spell to shine. In climbing-trips.csv as Llanberis.

venues.name: Isle of Arran
venues.why: Coarse-grained granite ridges and slabs rising straight from the sea — Cir Mhor's clean walls are the prize, and the Glasgow-ferry approach makes it feel like an expedition. Bring midge protection and a sense of humour about bog. In climbing-trips.csv as Aaran.

venues.name: Picos de Europa
venues.why: Alpine limestone in green northern Spain — the huge orange faces of the Naranjo de Bulnes are the objective, with a mountain hut right below the wall. Far cooler than southern Spain in late July, though Atlantic mists can roll in without notice. In climbing-trips.csv.

venues.name: Paklenica (Anica Kuk)
venues.why: Velebit's big limestone canyon above the Adriatic: routes to 350m on Anica Kuk, bolted belays, and a swim across the road afterwards. Workable in July if you chase shade — the great NORTH face of Anica Kuk stays out of the sun and most crags face E/W. Season Apr–Oct (sources: climbgrades.com, mojagear.com).

venues.name: Donegal (Sail Rock)
venues.style: sea cliffs & sea stacks (Cnoc na Mara)
venues.why: Ireland's wild west: Sail Rock's perfect corner lines and the Cnoc na Mara sea-stack are as adventurous as trad gets, three hours' drive from Belfast. Remote, free and swell-dependent — a calm, dry window here beats almost anywhere.
venues.hub: drive from Belfast (~3h); Michel flies to Belfast

venues.name: West Cornwall (Bosigran)
venues.why: Golden granite above the Atlantic — Commando Ridge, Doorpost and Chair Ladder's tiered walls are England's finest sea-cliff trad. It is the warmest, driest corner of the UK in late July, and the rock dries in minutes in a breeze.

venues.name: Écrins (Aiguille Dibona)
venues.why: The Dibona is the prettiest rock spire in the Alps — a clean granite blade with classic routes on every face and a comfortable hut an hour below. High-pressure late-July weather is the norm, and climbing at 3,000m keeps the heat civilised.

venues.name: Stetind (Norway)
venues.why: Norway's national mountain: a 1,392m granite obelisk rising straight out of the fjord, with the classic Sydpillaren pillar as the prize. Late July means midnight sun — 24 hours of daylight and no bivvy stress — so weather is the only gatekeeper.
venues.hub: fly via Oslo to Evenes (Narvik) + drive
